title: Russian Refineries Hit 21-Year Low, EU Buys Record Russian LNG, and OPEC Cuts Demand Again
subhead: Ukrainian drone strikes have gutted Russian refining to the lowest output since 2005, forcing a diesel export ban that is tightening global fuel markets — even as the EU buys record volumes of Russian LNG ahead of its 2027 ban, and OPEC trims its demand forecast for the third straight month.

sections.heading: Russian Refining at 21-Year Low as Ukraine's Drone Campaign Devastates Domestic Fuel Supply
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone strikes have pushed Russian crude-processing rates to 3.91 million barrels per day so far in July, the lowest level since March 2005 and more than 1.4 million bpd below year-ago levels, according to EA Analytics data cited by Bloomberg. The IEA's own estimate puts June processing even lower at 3.8 million bpd, a drop of 1.6 million bpd from a year earlier.

Over the past 100 days, Ukrainian forces have carried out roughly 50 attacks on fuel infrastructure, hitting at least 24 of Russia's 34 large refineries, a Bloomberg tally confirms. This month, drones reached the Omsk refinery, one of Russia's largest, which mainly serves the domestic market. Every one of Russia's 10 largest refineries has been struck in 2026, with damage estimated at 20–42% of total refining capacity, according to CryptoBriefing.

The result is visible on Russian streets: multiple regions from Kaliningrad to the Pacific Coast report fuel shortages, hours-long lines at gas stations, and spiking pump prices. Some areas have introduced rationing by license plate, and the Novosibirsk region recommended companies reinstate remote work to reduce fuel consumption. Deputy PM Alexander Novak acknowledged 'problems and shortages' in local media. Russian search engine Yandex recorded over 17,000 searches for 'how to make gasoline' in one month — the highest since the 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has turned Russia's own energy infrastructure into a strategic vulnerability. The drone campaign is not just degrading military logistics — it is cratering Russia's domestic fuel supply and export revenue simultaneously. Russia's ability to sustain its war economy depends on refining capacity that no longer exists at scale.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports as Global Fuel Markets Tighten
sections.body:
Russia imposed a temporary ban on most diesel exports on July 8, following earlier restrictions on gasoline and jet fuel shipments. As the world's second-largest diesel exporter after the US, Russia's withdrawal from the market has immediate consequences: US diesel crack spreads widened from just over $60 per barrel in late June to more than $80 on the day the ban was announced, according to the Korea Times.

South Korean refiners are emerging as key beneficiaries. Korea, the world's fifth-largest refining nation with 3.2 million bpd of capacity, exported 188 million barrels of petroleum products in the first five months of 2026, with diesel comprising 40.6% of exports. Analyst Kang Jin-hyeok of Shinhan Investment & Securities noted that 'the sharp decline in Russia's refining capacity, coupled with the diesel export ban, is likely to support refining margins for a while.' Shares of SK Innovation jumped 7.09% and S-Oil gained 5.6% on Monday, sharply outperforming the KOSPI.

The global diesel squeeze is compounded by the Strait of Hormuz disruptions, which have idled significant Middle East refining capacity. Two of the world's largest diesel suppliers — Russia and the Gulf states — are simultaneously operating below capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: The diesel market is where energy security meets economic reality. Diesel powers trucks, ships, construction, and agriculture. When the second-largest exporter pulls out and the Gulf can't fully compensate, the price signal travels fast through every supply chain.

sections.heading: EU Buys Record Russian LNG Despite Looming 2027 Ban
sections.body:
The European Union imported a record 9.97 million metric tons of Russian LNG worth approximately €5.96 billion ($6.82 billion) from the Yamal LNG facility in the first half of 2026, a 16% increase over the same period in 2025, according to Kpler data reported by Reuters and OilPrice. Overall EU imports of Russian LNG rose 11% year-on-year, while Russian pipeline gas imports increased 7%.

Of 140 Yamal LNG cargoes shipped globally in H1 2026, 136 went to EU ports, per an MSN analysis — nearly 97% of total output. France, Belgium, and Spain are the largest buyers, while Hungary remains the top pipeline gas customer via TurkStream.

The purchases are front-loading ahead of a complete Russian LNG ban set for January 1, 2027, under the REPowerEU Gas Regulation. The EU's short-term LNG ban took effect April 25, 2026, but exemptions and long-term contracts have allowed the surge to continue. The irony is stark: Europe is paying Putin roughly $6.8 billion in six months for gas even as G7 leaders discuss further sanctions against Moscow.

Meanwhile, Asia's LNG imports are recovering, drawing cargoes away from Europe. The Star (Malaysia) reports that Asian LNG imports are poised to hit a six-month high in July, while European imports plunge to their lowest in nearly two years.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's energy independence remains a work in progress. The record LNG imports reveal the gap between political declarations and physical energy reality — the EU is still funding Russia's war economy even as it prepares to cut the cord. The January 2027 ban will test whether Europe can actually replace those volumes.

sections.heading: OPEC Cuts Demand Forecast for Third Straight Month as Supply Rebounds Faster Than Consumption
sections.body:
OPEC trimmed its 2026 global oil demand growth forecast by 190,000 bpd to 780,000 bpd, the third consecutive monthly downgrade, according to its July monthly report. Even so, OPEC's forecast remains more bullish than the IEA's. The group also raised its 2027 demand growth estimate by 210,000 bpd to 1.94 million bpd, betting on a recovery in the second half.

On the supply side, OPEC+ June output climbed roughly 3 million bpd from May to average 36.28 million bpd, but this was not new capacity — it was stored oil finally moving through the reopening Strait of Hormuz. The UAE, post-OPEC exit, pumped a record 4.1 million bpd in June and ramped up exports through Fujairah. The US is producing nearly 14 million bpd. Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, and Iran are all bringing output back online.

The problem: supply is recovering faster than demand. OPEC's own analysis acknowledges that easing geopolitical tensions could strengthen H2 growth, but shipping through Hormuz remains well below pre-war levels, insurance costs are elevated, and fresh military strikes continue to threaten regional infrastructure. OilPrice notes the group 'spent much of this year unable to produce what it wanted because Hormuz was effectively closed. The next problem may come from producing everything it wants in a market that wants a little less.'
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC's third consecutive downgrade is a signal: the market's problem is shifting from supply shortage to demand weakness. The cartel that spent months struggling to get oil to market now faces the opposite risk — too many barrels chasing too few buyers, especially if the global economy slows.

sections.heading: Ukraine Shuts Down the Sea of Azov, Choking Russian Grain and Fuel Exports
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone forces have forced Russia to completely halt shipping in the Sea of Azov, according to Reuters. Between July 6 and July 13, Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces struck more than 100 Russian vessels — including seven tankers, five bulk carriers, one ferry, and two tugboats in a single overnight operation. Ukraine's drone commander reported hitting 15 Russian vessels overnight on July 13 alone.

The campaign has driven nearly all large Russian ships out of the Sea of Azov, with the exception of about 25 vessels clustered in the northeast. Most have fled to the Black Sea. The Institute for the Study of War assesses that Ukraine is now in a 'new phase' of operations aimed at isolating occupied Crimea from the Russian logistics network.

The impact extends beyond fuel. Reuters reports that the Azov shutdown could affect one-quarter of Russian grain exports, and wheat prices are already rising. Russia is the world's largest grain exporter, and the disruption of a quarter of its shipments comes at a critical point in the global food supply calendar.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has demonstrated that a country without a conventional navy can effectively blockade a major maritime corridor using drones alone. The Sea of Azov shutdown is both a military and economic escalation — it directly threatens Russia's grain export revenue and Crimean supply lines, and it validates the drone-as-naval-power model that Iran has also exploited in Hormuz.

sections.heading: Lithium Leads All Commodities in H1 2026 as AI and Grid Storage Drive Demand Shift
sections.body:
Lithium was the top-performing commodity in the first half of 2026, gaining more than 22%, according to Forbes. The rally is fundamentally different from the 2022 EV-driven bubble: this time, the demand driver is stationary energy storage and AI data centers, not electric vehicles.

EV sales in the US actually fell more than 20% year-on-year in Q2, per Cox Automotive, as federal tax credits expired. Instead, demand for grid-scale storage batteries surged 51% in 2025, roughly double the growth rate of EV batteries. JPMorgan expects storage to account for 30% of global lithium demand this year, rising to 36% by 2030. Albemarle, the world's largest producer, projects total lithium demand will roughly double to 3.7 million tonnes by the end of the decade.

UNCTAD projects lithium demand to rise 353% between 2024 and 2040, with supply concentrated in a handful of countries and nearly 100 new export restrictions introduced since 2020. Meanwhile, China's Xi Jinping signed agreements targeting Namibia's oil, uranium, and critical minerals during a state visit, as Windhoek pushed for domestic processing rather than raw-material extraction — part of a broader African trend toward resource nationalism.
sections.whyItMatters: The lithium rally signals a structural shift in energy demand — the AI buildout is becoming a minerals story, not just a compute story. With nearly 100 export restrictions imposed since 2020 and African nations demanding domestic processing, supply chain security for battery materials is becoming a geopolitical issue on par with oil.

sections.heading: India's Uranium Diplomacy and the Indo-Pacific Nuclear Shuffle
sections.body:
Prime Minister Modi's July visits to Australia and New Zealand elevated bilateral ties and advanced India's nuclear energy partnerships. MSN reports that India's uranium diplomacy with Australia has become a focal point, as New Delhi seeks to secure fuel supplies for its expanding nuclear fleet — part of a broader Indo-Pacific recalibration that includes South Korea, Japan, Indonesia, and Australia under what analysts call the 'Mandala framework.'

The push comes as nuclear power gains renewed strategic relevance: India operates 23 nuclear reactors with plans for 10 more, and securing Australian uranium supply is central to that expansion. The Firstpost analysis notes that Modi's trip 'strengthened India's energy and investment future' in the Indo-Pacific.

China's parallel move — signing agreements targeting Namibia's uranium and critical minerals — illustrates the competitive dynamic. Both Asian powers are racing to lock in fuel for nuclear and clean energy, with Africa and the Indo-Pacific as the main arenas.
sections.whyItMatters: Uranium is joining lithium and rare earths as a strategic mineral. India and China are pursuing parallel fuel-supply diplomacy, and Australia's role as a uranium supplier is becoming a lever in Indo-Pacific alignment. The nuclear renaissance is now a foreign policy tool.

bottomLine: Two major energy supply chains are being disrupted simultaneously by military action: Russia's refining sector is at a 21-year low, and the Sea of Azov — carrying a quarter of Russian grain exports — has been effectively closed by Ukrainian drones. The diesel export ban and global fuel squeeze are immediate consequences. Meanwhile, the EU is paradoxically buying record volumes of Russian LNG even as it prepares to ban them, and OPEC's third straight demand downgrade suggests the market's problem is shifting from too little supply to too much. Lithium's emergence as the top commodity — driven by AI and grid storage rather than EVs — marks the beginning of a demand structure that barely existed three years ago.
sources: Bloomberg (via RigZone), Reuters, OilPrice, Forbes, Ars Technica, Korea Times, Yonhap, CryptoBriefing, IEA, OPEC, Kpler, EA Analytics, Institute for the Study of War, Firstpost, MSN, FT (via Yahoo/Syndication), Cox Automotive, UNCTAD, JPMorgan, Albemarle, CNN (via CBS News)
